paragraphs.text: Payment processors, as a de-facto monopoly, should not be able to use their market position to pressure vendors worldwide to remove products which are legal in the country of origin and the governing law of these entities. They should operate similarly to the principle of net neutrality and only deny services when asked by an official government entity in the places they operate in. Especially considering that morality is not a protected legal interest in a modern rule of law and therefore not accessible as grounds for restrictions and censorship. Using loop-holes such as deeming products "risky" for their brand should be void as that makes no sense given their position as a tool/utility for other companies. Nobody blames the makers of creative tools that people produce controversial content either. In a free and fair market every legal product must be accessible without giving up ones privacy and therefore crippling markets. No fictional media justifies the establishment of a surveillance state and the imprisonment over thoughts (and by extension fantasies). This therefore should mean; no mandatory age-verification systems as that violates multiple fundamental rights and is not compatible with a democracy and its values (Study conducted for the EU-parliament: "A risk-based evaluation of available and upcoming age assurance technologies from a fundamental rights perspective.", February 2024). As lawmakers you should also keep reminding yourself that public outcry over certain content is not grounds for stricter and harsher legislation, or the lack of necessary regulation as outlined above. There should be a reliable voice of reason when morality is repeatedly being cited as the predominant reason for the introduction of criminal law, censorship and other measures.
